text: The laparoscopic procedure begins with the establishment of pneumoperitoneum using a needle to achieve a pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury. The optical view entry provides a clear visualization of the abdominal cavity for the nascent fundoplication addressing a large sliding hiatus hernia.

The surgeon places two secondary ports on either side of the optical entry in the supraumbilical region, approximately twenty centimeters from the xiphoid process, for working instruments. An additional port is positioned laterally in the left axillary line to facilitate retraction of the stomach.
In the epigastric region, a self-made three millimeter Nathanson liver retractor is positioned to elevate the liver, providing a clear view of the hiatus area.
The surgical team positions the patient in a steep head-up orientation, with the surgeons standing between the patient's legs, following the placement of laparoscopic ports.
The surgeon uses Hunter Graspers to manipulate and pull down the contents of the large hernia defect, aiming to completely remove the hernia sac from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic ACE device to carefully dissect through Kushner's membrane, an avascular gastrohepatic membrane, revealing the location of the right crux.
The surgeon sacrifices the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve while removing the complete sac, noting the patient's previous cholecystectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the crux, avoiding penetration into the muscle fibers, while aiming to remove the complete sac to prevent seroma formation.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum on both the right and left crura, taking small bites to avoid injuring the esophagus during the procedure.
The surgeon reaches the top of the two crura and attempts to excise the complete hernia sac, placing it into an endo bag for removal.
The surgeon performs dissection both anterior and posterior to the esophagus, progressing towards the mediastinal level, carefully separating tissue layers around the esophagus.
The surgeon ensures the complete dissection of the hernia sac from surrounding tissues, maintaining hemostasis throughout the process.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the anterior part of the esophagus, taking care to avoid injury to the vagus nerve, which is visible in close proximity to the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the left crus of the diaphragm and begins mobilizing the short gastric vessels, carefully dissecting them to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon uses a Harmonic ACE device to mobilize the short gastric vessels, reaching the apex of the two crura and ensuring that the attachments between the esophagus and stomach are freed.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the surgical area, ensuring that all attachments of the esophagus and stomach are freed, with hemostasis maintained.
The surgeon ensures an adequate length of the esophagus is achieved before creating a retroesophageal window, carefully maneuvering around the esophagus to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully avoids working too close to the left crus, leaving a layer of fibro fatty tissue intact on the crus during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the esophagus, both intra-abdominally and intra-thoracically up to the mediastinum, before proceeding to create a retroesophageal window.
The surgeon carefully navigates the retroesophageal space, taking care to avoid injuring the posterior branch of the vagus nerve.
During the dissection, the posterior vagus nerve and right vagus nerve are visible, positioned close to the esophagus, and are left undisturbed in their natural proximity to the esophagus.
The surgeon reaches the gastroesophageal (GE) junction and frees it from surrounding tissues, creating a retroesophageal window.
The surgeon performs bouginage using a gastric calibration tube, likely to assist in the dilation or measurement of the esophagus during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the retroesophageal dissection, revealing the two crura. The dissection proceeds retroesophageal rather than retrogastric, maintaining visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon places a 15-centimeter umbilical tape around the esophagus in a retroesophageal position, avoiding a retrogastric approach.
The surgeon places two or three square knots using umbilical tape to retract the esophagus downward, utilizing a port positioned on the left axillary line.
The surgeon uses a retroesophageal tape to free fibro fatty tissue adhesions from the esophagus, achieving complete mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the esophagus, ensuring no injury occurs to the anterior and posterior branches of the vagus nerve during the retroesophageal dissection.
The surgeon removes the complete hernia sac and proceeds to perform crurography, suturing the crura to reinforce the diaphragm.
The surgeon mobilizes the right and left crura, using a non-absorbable polyester suture number two to secure the structures in place.
The surgeon uses a 45 mm taper point needle to approximate the left and right crura with a U stitch, ensuring precise alignment and preventing tearing of the crural fibers.
The surgeon places three sutures to achieve complete approximation of the two crura, ensuring precise alignment and closure.
The surgeon performs krurography, using non-absorbable polyester sutures to approximate the left and right crura, ensuring precise alignment and minimizing muscle tearing.

The surgeon performs posterior crurography, focusing on suturing the posterior aspect of the diaphragmatic crura without involving the anterior portion, to avoid potential obstruction or strangulation of the esophagus.
The surgeon tests the repair by passing a bougie through the esophageal space to ensure it is not overly tightened.
The surgeon places a stay suture at the fundus of the stomach, securing it in position.
The surgeon uses a silk suture to secure the highest portion of the stomach fundus, placing the stay suture posterior to the gastrolytic area and leaving it six inches in length.
The surgeon uses the silk suture as a guide to perform the wrap around the stomach fundus, ensuring proper placement and tension during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon places a six-inch suture on the posterior part of the stomach, preparing to go retroesophageal and manipulate the fundus.
The surgeon pulls the suture on the right side to ensure there is no twisting of the stomach, performing the shoe shine maneuver to adjust the position of the stomach fundus comfortably.
The surgeon ensures that the right portion of the stomach wrap remains securely positioned and does not slip off the retroesophageal area.
The surgeon places a serocerosal, gastrogastric suture to secure the stomach in position, ensuring proper alignment and stability during the procedure.
The surgeon secures the wrap by including the esophagus in the first suture, ensuring the wrap remains stable and prevents slippage during the postoperative period.
The surgeon carefully places polyester or 2-0 Ethibond sutures to avoid causing injury to the liver during the procedure.
The surgeon gently pulls the two structures together, likely involving the esophagus and stomach, as part of the suturing process during the wrap formation.
The surgeon places three sutures to create a wrap around the right and left portions of the stomach, forming a structure approximately three to four centimeters in length.
The surgeon positions the gastric wrap around the esophagus, ensuring it is placed retroesophageally.
The surgeon adjusts the wrap to ensure it is a floppy wrap, allowing for flexibility and avoiding tension around the esophagus.
The surgeon completes gastro-gastric suturing with three sutures, then secures the right portion of the wrap to the right crus to prevent slippage.
The surgeon performs right cruropexy followed by left cruropexy to secure the wrap and prevent its migration in the post-operative period.
The surgeon removes all the laparoscopic ports under direct visualization, ensuring careful extraction from the surgical site.
